speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three with Ralph Fischer. What happened to members of your immediate family?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I was deported in '41, they all left the ghetto-- my brother, two sisters, and my parents. They were all alive. I didn't know nothing. When I was in Posen camp, sometimes there were Poles who came from Lodz and told me about a ghetto, that they killed the Jews. Of course, my heart was very, very, very aching. I had no idea what happened to them. And I still didn't know till the end, till I came back to Lodz, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my parents lived till 1944, and also-- no, my sister was deported before. Because she had a little child. And I don't know how-- that's what told me. She was deported before with the child. But my parents-- my, oh, my brother didn't ever make the ghetto. My brother was a dentist. And he was the Polish army. I never seen him since 1940, 1939, I never seen him. I don't know what happened to him. And my parents, they went to Auschwitz with my, with my, with my little sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met, after the war, a girlfriend of hers. She was with her in camp in Auschwitz. And she told me my mother died right away, she says, maybe two, four weeks. Her, her heart broke or something. And my sister was alive about a year or two. And she died of typhus. So that's what I know. That's the [? latest. ?] That's what I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to add something which I forgot. It's very important. In the terrible camp, Gutenbrunn, one Sunday came the Gestapo with four Jews from a different camp. And they hung them. We had to stay and look at them. And they were hanged by the Jewish police. They had to do it. And there was one Gestapo man-- in that time, we worked with the Poles. We had money. We could buy something. If you had money, you could buy something. And even, we stand in appell, he come over to everybody, give me your money. So some gave him, some didn't. He comes to me. He look at me give me your money. Give me [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I have no money. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], he says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I says, I don't have it. Ah. He passed by. Every few weeks-- his name was [? Fifi ?]. He was a little small guy. He must have been a [NON-ENGLISH], because he spoke Yiddish. Every few weeks, the Gestapo came with two, four people. If this not enough for the, for the, for the ropes, they pick two people from, from us. Usually, pick people with ethnic features-- a big nose or dark-- and hung him. And we  had to look at it. Yeah, that's what I forgot to tell you, imagine. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] One day, also-- I forgot to tell you-- this was [? Stefansson ?] also at camp Posen. I passed by a, a a with potatoes. I climbed and fill up my-- my pants was tied-- and fill up the pants with potatoes. When I went down, there was two [? Schupa, ?] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], waiting for me. They took me to the precinct. And there was a few SS men. Ah!

speaker: interviewer
text: Say, one says, you know what? As many potatoes you have, so many lashes on your blank behind you're going to get. And they put me on a table. One sit on my back. And I got 44 lashes. I was screaming my head off. Then I said, maybe I'll be psychological. I will hear, mama, mama, maybe this will help. Maybe they're human. That didn't help. They beat the hell out of me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I swell up right away. And I had to go back to work. After beating, I have to go back to work. Walking back to work, I crossed the same wagon. And it shows you what-- we're not human. I climbed again and pick up some potatoes. This time, I, I make it to the camp. We are not humans, not all times. There were times I was wondering. I forgot the faces of my parents. Food, food-- this was the time I was hungry. But I was lucky enough not to be hungry a long time. Then let me-- [? talk about ?] some facts, which are worth, worth mention it. Yeah, this was Ebensee. I work at night shift. And I worked for German meister. And I don't know why he picked me. He says, come to my-- he had a shed-- and see that the fire doesn't go out. Because it's winter. But coal, I used to steal. Yes, that's your problem. I have the coal. So, I, I I managed to steal some coal and keep that shed warm. And I was warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened, some, some prisoners, Jews, came in to me to warm themselves up-- three, four, five, six, seven. And one day, the German is seeing it. He says, I don't want nobody here. And they came. And I said, get out of here. You can't be here. It didn't help. I couldn't push them out. Every time I begged them, no. And I couldn't-- it was cold outside. One day, he came. He see again the Jews. He kicked me out like them. And I was forced to get away-- and to work and be cold again. But what ca-- what can you do? They were blue. And I was warm, nice [? and saucy ?]. But I paid with a job. Do you still believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very mad with God. After I saw the children I said, God, if you exist you are a sadist. How can you see innocent little babies-- you bring them to the world. And now you let them exterminate like, like roaches or ants. Oh no, I cursed his name. I cursed his name. But I raised my kids to believe in God. A matter of fact, my son went to the yeshiva for a few years. I go to the shul because of tradition. And my kids-- my son belongs to two shuls, even. My daughter belongs to a shul. Let them be Jew. Because I believe that never again-- hopefully, as I see the world, it, it, it happens again, or not at that format, not at that cataclysm. Look at Bosnia and Herzegovina, what's going on. That was my lesson-- my lesson that the biggest animal of all animals, animals, is the human animal.

speaker: interviewer
text: I couldn't believe in a, in a people like Germany, the, them the nation of Goethe, [? Schuyler, ?] Beethoven, could be so barbaric. Oh, we say, the Russians or the Poles, yeah. But them? So there's no logic in anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Shall I remember something else, if I do? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What can I tell you? One day-- yes, one day, it was at Gutenbrunn. The Gestapo came with only two Jews to hang. And they needed seven. And there was a Jewish policeman, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. When he passed by to pick the victims, he chose me. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the German comes over. He says no, not with such, such blue eyes, not [INAUDIBLE] such a blue eyes. He, he went away. If he, if he, if he wouldn't approach, he look at me, I would have hang with the other ones. No, your life was worth nothing. Then I said, I said to the [? Hirchela ?], if I find you after the war, I going to kill you. Because you wanted to kill me. I never see him again. I think he died someplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for your patience. My testimony, I want my children to know about it-- whoever. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The man in the center is Betsalel Ficher, my father. On the left side you have Uncle Joe, on the other side Uncle Sam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those are his brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is his brother-in-laws. He had two sisters who married two brothers. That take-- that photo was taken in Patterson, New Jersey, in 1912. My father lived in the United States for two years. 1914, he had a bright idea to go home for a bride. And then we got stuck and waited for Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a-- this is a picture that was made in Poland in-- I would say I was about four years old-- 1928, '29, something like this. We went, we went to the country. And it was made over there. In the center is my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name? His name was Betsalel. On the right hand side is me, Ralph Fischer. My mother sitting over there, to the left. Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosa, [? Circarosa, ?] actually I called her. And this was [? Manya ?], or Miriam, my sister. The other lady is our neighbor. Her name was Radoshivski. That little guy, his name was Natek. He died in the ghetto. She died in France, his mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Left hand side, my mother-- bless her memory. She was a wonderful woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to her-- her name was the [? Circarosa. ?] Next to her was my, my aunt. Her name was Helen. Next to her was my younger aunt. Her name was Gutcha. Gutcha actually raised me, because she was a young girl. She came from a little town to the big town, Lodz, and stayed with us. And that was [? a ?] [? big ?] [? kindness ?], and she raised me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken, oh, I would say, 1918, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Zawiercie, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Miriam [? Manusha ?] Fischer, my little sister. It was made in Lodz, that picture. The year would be, I would say, '38-- something like that. What happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died in the Holocaust. I don't know exactly where-- yes, in Auschwitz. Because I spoke to her girlfriends. They remembered her. She was a very bright child, pretty-- never lived to became, a, a, a wife or a mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, here on the left side is Ralph Fischer, which is me. In the center is Natek Radowiski, a neighbor. On the right hand side is Monek Radowiski. It was made in Lodz in 1924. Natek died in the ghetto. And Monek lives still in Paris, France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, left hand side, my, my lovely bride, wife, Rosette, center, Claude. And on the right, it's me. I look young then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ralph Fischer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 194-- 1949, I would say. 1949, in Paris, France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, left hand side, Ralph Fischer, in center, my son Claude, on the right hand side, Rosette, my wife. That picture was taken in 1950, I would say, on the Champs-Elysees in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is the latest addition to, to the Fischer family. On the left hand side is my daughter, Cathy. And on the other side is Peter, her husband. His name is Bloch. I'd like to mention, he's the great grandson of Ernest Bloch, which is a Jewish composer famous for his and They got married about two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles, in the Sephardic Temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, here's my pride and joy. This is my son, Claude, his wife, Ann-- and [? Swidler ?]. The picture was taken in Washington DC. And this is their wedding night. My son is a professor at Berkeley University. So is Ann, a professor at Berkeley University. And they gave me two nice, beautiful grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their marriage was, I would say-- now it's '96-- '76, 20 years ago-- 20 years ago, yes. That's when--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is Abraham-- or Avi as we call him-- Fischer, my grandson. The picture was taken in Berkeley, California, about three years ago, which will be 1993. He just had bar mitzvah. He had a very nice bar mitzvah in Berkeley.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, this is Leah. She is a tomboy. She is-- now, she's seven years old. And the picture was taken in Berkeley, New Jer-- California. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- two years ago. It would be 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose daughter is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's Claude's daughter, and my granddaughter.
